front: Composer: coined the term Gesamtkunstwerk ('total work of art') in essays 'Art and Revolution' and 'The Artwork of the Future'; developed the concept of the leitmotif in 'Opera and Drama'
Composer: wrote the antisemitic essay 'Jewishness in Music,' attacking Giacomo Meyerbeer and Felix Mendelssohn

Composer: Friedrich Nietzsche praised him in The Birth of Tragedy but later attacked him in 'The Case of Wagner' and 'Nietzsche Contra Wagner,' calling him a 'symptom of nihilism' and 'Cagliostro of modernity'
Composer: father-in-law was Franz Liszt (Cosima was Liszt's daughter); son-in-law Houston Stewart Chamberlain wrote The Foundations of the Nineteenth Century
Composer: designed the Wagner tuba to bridge the gap between French horns and trombones; Bruckner first used it symphonically in his seventh symphony
Composer: Bruckner dedicated his Third Symphony to him and included a cymbal crash representing his death in the seventh symphony; Bruckner's Third liberally quotes his operas
Composer: buried at the estate 'Wahnfried' (meaning 'peace from delusion') next to his Newfoundland dog Russ; Leonard Bernstein joked his grave was just large enough to dance upon
Composer: George Bernard Shaw wrote 'The Perfect Wagnerite' (Marxist interpretation of the Ring); Alex Ross wrote Wagnerism: Art and Politics in the Shadow of Music (2020)
Composer: 'Ride of the Valkyries' underscores the helicopter attack in Apocalypse Now; Lohengrin prelude used in The Great Dictator for Hynkel's globe-dance
Opera: features the 'Tristan chord' (F, B, D-sharp, G-sharp) — dissonant and left unresolved until the climactic love-death scene
Opera: opens with cellos playing an eighth A up to a long F, then eighth E, long D-sharp, before the dissonant title chord
Opera: ends with Isolde's 'Liebestod' (Mild und leise) — she asks King Marke and Brangäne if she sees how her deceased lover glows 'softly and gently'
Opera: Debussy called it 'decidedly the finest thing I know'; Debussy humorously quoted its opening chord in 'Golliwog's Cakewalk' (marked 'avec une grande émotion')
Opera: approximately 16-hour, four-part cycle (Das Rheingold, Die Walküre, Siegfried, Götterdämmerung); leitmotifs guide plot connections; Hans von Wolzogen coined their common nicknames
work: Der Ring des Nibelungen (The Ring Cycle)

front: Opera: the Siegfried leitmotif is a French horn call; some leitmotifs originated from the Siegfried Idyll, written for Cosima's birthday
work: Der Ring des Nibelungen (The Ring Cycle)

front: Opera: George Bernard Shaw's 'The Perfect Wagnerite' is a Marxist interpretation of it; Patrice Chéreau's 1976 centenary Bayreuth production was Shaw-inspired socialist realism
work: Der Ring des Nibelungen (The Ring Cycle)

front: Opera: Wagner's only mature comedy, over four-and-a-half hours; Hans Sachs coaches the protagonist to win a song contest; villain Beckmesser was originally named 'Hanslich' after critic Eduard Hanslick
Opera: densely contrapuntal C-major overture; aria 'Wahn! Wahn! Überall Wahn!' laments a riot after the villain's interrupted serenade

Opera: song contest at Wartburg between the title character and Wolfram von Eschenbach; includes 'Pilgrims' Chorus'; title character's staff sprouts leaves at end; Paris premiere disrupted by noisemakers
Opera: Telramund accuses Elsa of Brabant of murder; Elsa dies after asking the title knight his name; includes the 'Bridal Chorus'
Opera festival: dedicated to this composer's works in Bavaria; Winifred Wagner gave the Nazi party control during WWII; 'Ride of the Valkyries' is its most famous excerpt
